help.translation: "I can't do this anymore.
I'm just not strong enough."
help.note: 〜なんだ (= 〜のだ) frames the line as a confession or explanation rather than a flat statement. Combined with 力不足 ("insufficient strength") it lands as "the truth is, I don't have what it takes."

help.translation: I plead with the members of 《Lamenting Spectres》— my childhood friends — in an agonized voice.
help.note: The Japanese has no stated subject; it is understood to be the narrator ("I"). 《嘆きの亡霊》 is the party's name and the title of the book.

help.translation: A year had gone by since I'd started working as a Hunter.
It was just one year, but for me it had been nothing short of tumultuous.
Treasuries.
Living "Phantoms" born of mana material that has piled up and piled up; vicious monsters that take up residence there hungering for power.
help.note: This and the next sentence are noun-fragment vignettes (体言止め) rather than full sentences — a literary listing rhythm that paints a Treasury in brushstrokes instead of describing it in prose. 溜まりに溜まった repeats the verb (stem + に + 〜た form) for emphasis: "piled up upon piled up."

help.translation: Countless malicious traps strung throughout — and even your fellow Treasure Hunters can't be counted on as allies.
help.note: 〜だって here = 〜でも ("even"), casual register. 〜とは限らない is a stock phrase: "it is not necessarily the case that…" — softening a flat claim into a hedge.

help.translation: I'd heard the stories.
help.note: 話には聞いていた is an idiomatic opener: "I'd heard about it (in the abstract) — but…". The は flags an implicit contrast with the speaker's actual experience, which the next sentence will deliver.

help.translation: I'd read book after book of past adventurers' tales — I thought I knew.
help.note: 〜ていたつもりだった always sets up a reversal: "I thought I (had/knew/was) — but actually I didn't." The next paragraph delivers the reversal.

help.translation: But the trials that confronted me once I'd actually become a Hunter shattered my soft, spoiled spirit with ease.
help.note: 甘ったれた根性 = "a coddled / spoiled spirit" — 甘ったれる is the verb for being indulged, and it's a self-deprecating word for a person who hasn't been toughened up by hardship.

help.translation: I lost count of how many times I nearly threw up.
help.note: 〜そうになる describes something that almost happened ("nearly did"). 何度〜かわからない ("I don't know how many times…") is a stock idiom meaning "countless times."

help.translation: A life of life-or-death adventures over and over had drained me to the bone, body and mind.
"I... I can't keep up with you all.
help.note: …… in dialogue marks a hesitation or break in the speaker's voice — translate as an ellipsis or a stammer rather than a literal pause.

help.translation: You all know it, don't you... that this party's progress through the Treasury is... falling behind because of me."
help.note: わかっているんだろう = "you know, don't you" — んだ + だろう seeks confirmation of an inferred truth. The sentence ends with the dangling object 〜という事を; the verb ("that you know") is held back to the previous clause, an inverted word order that sounds like the speaker trailing off after his point lands.

help.translation: Members of a Treasure Hunter party each have what's called a "role."
help.note: 〜というもの = "the thing called X" — frames "role" as a defined concept rather than a vague idea, the way you'd introduce a term to the reader.

help.translation: The "Attacker," who fights off monsters.
help.note: From here through p013 the narrator catalogues the party roles in noun-only fragments (体言止め) — labelling, not narrating.

help.translation: Swordsmen, mages.
help.note: 剣士 is given the reading ソードマン and 魔導師 as マギ — a fantasy-genre convention where Japanese kanji carry the meaning while katakana furigana give the loanword flavour. The kanji is what the reader "means"; the katakana is what they "hear."

help.translation: The "Support," who disarms gimmicks and scouts ahead.
Thieves, alchemists.
help.note: Same kanji-meaning / katakana-reading convention as p011: 盗賊 reads as シーフ and 錬金術師 as アルケミスト.

help.translation: The "Healer," who tends to the wounded and shields the party.
help.note: 〜たり (here 癒やしたり) lists representative actions out of many possible ones — "things like healing, and so on."

help.translation: Paladins.
help.note: 守護騎士 reads as パラディン — same kanji-as-meaning, katakana-as-sound convention.

help.translation: I had none of those skills — I was pure dead weight.
help.note: お荷物 literally "luggage," idiomatically "a burden / dead weight." どの〜も + ない = "none of (any of the) X."

help.translation: I did try.
help.note: 努力はした puts は on 努力 ("effort") to spotlight a contrast: "as for effort — yes, I made some" (… but it didn't pay off, the unspoken rest).

help.translation: I went and studied under any number of masters, casting around for anything I might have an aptitude for.
help.note: 僕だって = "even I" — the だって here is defensive, asserting the speaker's effort against an implied doubter.

help.translation: But when those without talent and those with it put in the same effort, that the latter grows stronger is plain as day.
help.note: 才なき is literary/archaic for 才のない ("without talent"). 自明の利 is the narrator's twist on 自明の理 ("a self-evident truth") — same sound, with 利 ("advantage") sneaking in the idea that talent isn't just clearer-cut, it pays better. The whole sentence ends as a noun phrase (体言止め), giving it the weight of an aphorism.

help.translation: And my childhood friends — Luke and the others — were astonishingly hard workers.
help.note: 〜達 attached to a personal name (ルーク達) means "Luke and the others (in his group)" — Japanese has no separate plural marker and uses this companion-suffix for groups associated with a named person.

help.translation: The kind of hard workers anyone watching would have called astonishing.
help.note: The repetition of 驚くほどの努力家だった across two sentences is deliberate — the second widens the lens from the narrator's view to anyone's. 誰が見ても = "no matter who's looking," a stock 〜ても construction.

help.translation: Given that everyone's day is only twenty-four hours long, there was no chance of me ever catching up to them.
help.note: 〜以上 (here しかない以上) means "so long as / given that ~" — it sets a fixed precondition the rest of the sentence reasons from. あり得ない is stronger than できない: not "I can't," but "it cannot exist as a possibility."

help.translation: A party is usually formed of five to six people.
If I weren't there — if they'd taken on one or two more comrades fitted to that party's level in my place — Luke and the others would have made it much further by now.
help.note: 〜ば〜たはずだ frames a counterfactual: how things should have gone if circumstances had differed. The dashed insertion ──...── carries the alternative scenario as a parenthetical revision. The whole sentence is the narrator condemning his own presence in the party.

help.translation: Hearing my words, Luke Saikol nodded with a grieved expression.
"Yeah... you're right, Cry — we don't have what it takes."
help.note: お前 here is gruff but affectionate among male friends, not aggressive. 俺達 + お前 + the missing copula 「だ」 carry the same casual masculine register as Luke's volitional+ぜ in chapter 0.
A wordless beat — just an ellipsis and a question mark. The narrator's confused reaction after Luke takes his self-deprecation at face value.

help.translation: "Sorry, Cry-chan.
help.note: ちゃん attached to a male name from a female speaker is affectionate and slightly teasing. ね softens the apology into something coaxing rather than formal.

help.translation: If only we were stronger, we wouldn't have made you worry like this..."
help.note: 〜ば〜のに is a counterfactual regret: "if X had been the case, Y wouldn't have happened — but X wasn't, so Y did." The trailing …… leaves the apology hanging.

help.translation: Liz Smart, the other comrade sitting beside him, agreed with the sentiment.
"No, that's not what—"
help.note: The dash ── cuts the speaker off mid-word: そうじゃな[い] ("that's not it") is interrupted before the final い. The narrator is talked over before he can correct them.

help.translation: They're strong.
Already strong to the point of being too strong.
help.note: 〜くらい marks the degree of a quality ("to the extent of ~"). The repeated 強い〜強い stacks the word three times across two sentences for rhetorical insistence.

help.translation: Even saddled with the dead weight that I am, they're strong enough to keep raising the level of the Treasuries they clear, one after another.
help.note: 〜ながらも = "even while doing X, still Y" — a concessive that forces a contrast between the two states. 僕という足手まとい literally "the dead weight that is me" — selfdeprecating attribution. どんどん is the onomatopoeia of relentless forward momentum.

help.translation: They'd have had a far easier time of it if the five of them had cleared it without me.
help.note: 〜たほうが楽だったはずだ = "it would surely have been easier to (have done) ~" — a regret-tinged counterfactual judgement.

help.translation: Letting my argument slip past him like wind through a willow, Luke spoke with a faraway look in his eyes.
help.note: 柳のように受け流す is an idiom: a willow bends with the wind without breaking — to receive and deflect a blow with no resistance. The narrator's plea simply doesn't land. 遠い目 = "a faraway look," eyes fixed on something not actually present.

help.translation: "This won't do, will it... haha... if we get bogged down in a place like this, the strongest Hunters? It's a dream of a dream.
help.note: こんなんじゃ (= こんなのでは) and 〜じゃ (= 〜では) are casual contractions matching Luke's gruff register. 夢のまた夢 = "a dream within a dream" — utterly impossible. 足止めを食う is an idiom: "to be held up / stalled."

help.translation: Thanks, man — you woke me right up.
help.note: ありがとよ (= ありがとう + よ) and sentence-final ぜ are the same casual masculine register Luke used in chapter 0. He is sincerely thanking the narrator for what he hears as a wake-up call — taking the self-deprecating speech as motivational.

help.translation: I'll go retrain from scratch — apprentice myself to that guy they call the Sword Saint or whatever..."
help.note: 〜てくる = "go (do X) and come back." わ at the end here is the gentler male assertion (not the feminine わ), giving a casual "I'll just go do it" feel. 剣聖とか呼ばれてる奴 ("the guy they call something like 'Sword Saint'") is deliberately offhand — Luke treats apprenticing under a legendary master like a quick errand.

help.translation: Luke set off to apprentice himself to the Sword Saint, whose name thundered across the imperial capital, with the breeziness of someone going for a walk.
help.note: ノリ (slang for "vibe / mood / energy") with 〜ようなノリで means "with the vibe of (doing X)." 〜なりに行った uses the 〜に行く motion-purpose construction ("go in order to ~").

help.translation: The others, too, each began floating their own plan to grow stronger.
help.note: 出していく combines 出す ("put forward") with the 〜ていく aspect ("going on (doing X)") — conveying a steady, ongoing rollout of plans, one after another.

help.translation: Listening to all this, I thought to myself.
These guys are hopeless.
help.note: Inverted, fragmentary order (predicate ダメだ first, then subject こいつら) is internal-monologue grammar — the way you'd actually think it, not the way you'd write it. こいつら is a gruff, impolite "these guys."

help.translation: They actually think that if they get stronger themselves, everything will sort itself out.
No matter how strong you all get, I'm going to stay weak.
help.note: いくら〜ても = "no matter how much…". 〜まま = "remaining in (the same state)." 君ら shifts from third-person こいつら to second-person "you (all)" — the narrator addresses his friends directly inside his head, even though they can't hear him.

help.translation: And so I cast about desperately for a way out of adventuring, and I hit on one good idea.
Let's build a clan.
help.note: 建てよう is the volitional of 建てる ("build") — the same "let's…" register as the なろう / 建てよう refrain that runs through chapter 0. The narrator is borrowing his friends' grammar of resolve, but this time he's deciding alone.

help.translation: Things can't continue as they are.
We'll just die, plain and simple.
help.note: 普通に here is colloquial filler — "normally / matter-of-factly" — and the casualness sharpens the morbid prediction rather than softening it.

help.translation: Even if we don't die, we'll go through hell.
help.note: 〜としても = "even if / even supposing." 死ぬような目に遭う is an idiom: 〜目に遭う means "to undergo (an unpleasant experience)" and 死ぬような目 = "a near-death experience."

help.translation: Let's build a clan.
help.note: Repeated verbatim from p033 — the doubled chant marks the moment the narrator commits to the plan.

help.translation: Let's build a clan together with other promising parties.
All to bring strong new members into 《Lamenting Spectres》.
help.note: 〜ために at sentence end with no main clause is a 体言止め-like rhetorical move — the purpose is left dangling as its own sentence, hammering home the goal.

help.translation: A fresh wind was needed.
To take a new step forward.
help.note: 新たなる is a slightly archaic / literary form of 新たな — gives the line an inflated, declamatory ring, like a manifesto. Mirrors the dangling 〜ために in p036.

help.translation: And so I founded 《First Steps》, and using the running of it as my excuse, I successfully escaped Treasury exploration.
help.note: 始まりの足跡 = "First Footsteps / Footprints of the Beginning," the clan's name. The narrator frames his act of founding the clan as a successful escape, not a noble undertaking — that ironic gap between his self-image and his friends' is the joke of the whole premise.

help.translation: That was three whole years ago now.
help.note: Mirrors the closing line of chapter 0 (もう五年も前の話だ) — same rhetorical beat, marking another sealed-off chapter of memory. The 〜も emphasises a quantity that feels surprisingly large.
